comment.body:
Very important question!
the key is, that: there is a collection of counterparties acting independently form each other, and the effect of their net activity aggregates. Different counterparties have different offsetting needs if there is any. But for us, traders, there is no edge on monitoring individual firms separately. 
I know that MMs’ risk exposures are fluid, often adjusted on a second-by-second basis, and so any snapshot is only a temporary state. Their profit comes from managing the bid/ask spread. 
...
The key is, again, that the firms inventory balance nets. 
And all what we are looking at is the net effect. This is all what we are interested in, and what matters from tape-reading perspective.
We are not MMs, or brokers to monitor their activities in every detailes.
SEC, CFTC obligates MM firms to be risk neutral, and to report aggregate datas (that one can buy)..

comment.body:
@marvin romanow  one can simply derive estimated gamma based on the reported volume data from exchanges. it is purchaseable.
the common assumption is that MMs hedge both regular and flex options, while firms only hedge regular ones. 
the difference is that regular options (listed on CBOE) have fixed strikes and expos and contract sizes, while on flex contract the customer can choose these terms. 
I always take both into account.

comment.body:
they are... yes... OD worked toghether with ex-CBOE MM to build its model.
i also have MM friends and quants that work for MM desks, ex-class mates, who helped me with it. 
of course, no 100% transparency. 
and also, sudden orders can hit anytime (tail events).
but, these estimations are good enough to work in a significant % of times, and thats what matters. 
..
however, this is one of the main reason I keep emphatizing that DEALER FLOW/GREEKS ARE NOT EVERYTHING.
this is only a fraction of the pic that a trader must see.
Read my educational posts, pls, you will see too what I mean by this. 
i don't like over-emphatizing greeks as it would be the only thing... it is a reflexive consequence. 
A trader who wants to frontrun the market looks at the momentums of the data shifts and think hours about the possible underlying reasons.

comment.body:
In simplified terms, its the same as when you trade on spot directions, but in vol trading, instead of the spot direction, you trade vol direction.. implied vol. 
For spot trading you mainly focus on the delta, gamma and charm of your contract,
in vol trading you watch vega, vomma and veta. 
+vanna for both, bcs the game is still three dimensional.
...
think in numbers. basically... the contract value changes for certain numbers in a certain way. the greeks: delta, vega, theta describe these changing behaviors. 
when you trade on spot, you speculate on the spot input changes on your contract. (bcs don't forget: YOU TRADE THE OPTION, NOT THE UNDERLYING!)
but when you trade on vol, you speculate on the vol input effects on your contract value.
this is the 'beauty' of options that you can short/long vol and short/long time.
...
shorting the smirk this time simply means that we bet on an overpricing... over-asked vol risk premium decay.
i will cover these things later in the year. vol trading is essential to understand market cycles

comment.body:
Very important question!
the key is, that: there is a collection of counterparties acting independently form each other, and the effect of their net activity aggregates. Different counterparties have different offsetting needs if there is any. But for us, traders, there is no edge on monitoring individual firms separately. 
I know that MMs’ risk exposures are fluid, often adjusted on a second-by-second basis, and so any snapshot is only a temporary state. Their profit comes from managing the bid/ask spread. 
...
The key is, again, that the firms inventory balance nets. 
And all what we are looking at is the net effect. This is all what we are interested in, and what matters from tape-reading perspective.
We are not MMs, or brokers to monitor their activities in every detailes.
SEC, CFTC obligates MM firms to be risk neutral, and to report aggregate datas (that one can buy)..

I just subscribed, and there’s a lot I don’t understand. Let me share some of my understanding—am I getting this right?

when there is a smrik , put iv is extremely high ，mm might short OTM put, long OTM call, to capture price discrepancy due to put being over-price, call under-price. 

this positions results in a positive delta exposure, to hedge this , mm need to short stocks to make overall position delta-neutral, when time /IV decrease, it will decrease the theta through vanna and charm effect,  so mm would need to buy back stocks, 0 vanna level means the short recover behavior  is stop or been weak, so it is act as a resistance level

and shork smrik may mean bet this price unbalance will disappear, bu i am no idea of how to achieve that…

comment.body: the market i think is unlikely to crash because of the debt ceiling. The debt ceiling has been reached so the Treasury cannot drain money from the economy by issuing bonds to finance its spending (thei is a zero sum game), so they have to spend money out of the TGA (TREASURY GENNERAL ACCOUNT) ...Government savings. That has about $800Bill in it,  enough to finance 4 months deficit. this money is usually stagnant in the economy sitting earning interest. It will now be spent into the economy...this is like QE without being QE...an extra $150B of liquidity every month until debt ceiling is raised.....then the bonds will be issued as normal but extra will have to be sold to refill the TGA.   tHE OTHER EFFECT WHEN NO BONDS ARE BEING SOLD IS IT HOLDS INTEREST RATES DOWN, AS THERE IS DEMAND FOR us DEBT BUT LIMITED SUPPLY WHEN DEBT CEILING IS IN PLACE.... so ONCE DEBT CEILING IS RAISED WE ARE MUCH MORE LIKELY TO SEE A CRASH IN MARKETS

comment.body:
Yes he would lose credibility. When he was campaigning, I don’t think he understood that he was constantly saying things that were diametrically opposed to each other. If you are going to drastically cut government spending, that will inevitably create downward pressure on asset prices and the economy. Yet, he was promising the strongest economy in the history of the world and has always referenced how the stock market was the strongest under his administration. He’s also, multiple times, been a champion of negative interest rates and has called for them during his last administration. So he’s got a lot of hypocrisy in his proposed policy vision. 

So my point is, the “powers that be” have a great opportunity to force his hand. And out of a necessity of survival, the “less of two evils” is for him to continue the “business as usual”, because holding true to decreasing government spending, on shoring jobs and increasing wages, would be too painful for the American people to absorb. Especially after the promises Trump has made.

comment.body:
Been reading Alma’s latest post a few times. Is this the gist you all are getting? Please let me know where I’m missing things. :::: 

Big players are hedging short term against large drops, while mid term they are shorting the skew (showing no real fear).  Because they do this while pushing bear narrative in media, they are setting the stage for a short term bear trap. After this current bear trap vol crushes spot up, insiders will distribute at ATH’s (maybe no new ATH if trendline isn’t broken, just repetitive, sell off, melt up, hence dangerous to trade long term right now).  

After this stage eventually we will see intentional sell off that will cause weak hands to panic sell, then the cash rich insiders will repurchase lowering their cost basis. 

Why? Because they want government sponsored capitalism running full steam again. A fed put ensuring victory in the upcoming economic war with china, and they get richer all the while.
